front: Empire: emerged from the predecessor kingdoms of Funan and Chenla
Empire: rulers held the titles chakravartin ("universal ruler") and devaraja ("god-king")
Empire: at its territorial height, conquered the Mon Kingdom and Grahi, with a western border at the Pagan Kingdom
Empire: historians describe its political structure with the term "mandala," in which the state tightly controlled its core territory but delegated frontier rule to vassals
Empire: flourished from the 9th to the 15th century
Ruler: the empire's founder declared independence from Java/the Sailendra dynasty around 802 CE
work: Founding: Jayavarman II & the Devaraja Cult

front: Ruler: established the devaraja "god-king" cult after receiving tutelage at a site on Phnom Kulen ("Lychee Mountain")
work: Founding: Jayavarman II & the Devaraja Cult

front: Ruler: absorbed the pre-existing Chenla people into the new empire
work: Founding: Jayavarman II & the Devaraja Cult

front: Ruler: moved his capital north of the Tonle Sap, removing the kingdom from the influence of Srivijaya
work: Founding: Jayavarman II & the Devaraja Cult

front: Document: the K. 235 inscription, a key source about this ruler, praises a god who "activates the senses of living beings"
work: Founding: Jayavarman II & the Devaraja Cult

front: Ruler: credited with establishing 121 "Houses of Fire" as rest stops for travelers
work: Founding: Jayavarman II & the Devaraja Cult

front: Ruler: the empire's most prolific early leader established a new capital named for the deity Harihara, where he built a namesake group of temples and monasteries
Temple: described as the world's largest religious complex
Temple: designed as a Vaishnavite complex modeled on Mount Meru, marking a shift from Shiva to Vishnu worship
Temple: features five towers symbolizing the peaks of Mount Meru
Temple: its Ramayana-inspired bas-reliefs depict an army led by its builder
Ruler: invaded Champa and restored diplomatic and commercial relations with China
Ruler: sent a jewel to the Chola Kingdom, then ruled by Kulottunga I, which had earlier fought the Tambralinga kingdom
Temple: appears on the flag of Cambodia
Temple: converted into a Buddhist site under King Jayavarman VII in the 12th century CE
Temple: King Norodom resumed renovation of it after returning to power in the 1990s
Temple: covered with "face towers" depicting a smiling king
Ruler: repeatedly depicted at his temple as the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara
Temple: bas-reliefs depict a naval battle against the neighboring Cham peoples
Temple: its towers were formerly gold-covered, per a Chinese diplomat's account
Ruler: patronized Mahayana Buddhism and revived it by building sites like Ta Prohm and Neak Pean
Site: the Terrace of the Leper King is located within this temple complex
Ruler: rebuilt the capital, restoring Angkor Wat, after it was sacked by the Chams
Institution: massive artificial reservoirs that irrigated the empire's land, fed by the Tonle Sap
Institution: their east-west orientation has led some scholars to suggest a spiritual, not just practical, purpose
Institution: two of them were built near a massive sandstone complex depicting the Churning of the Ocean of Milk
Institution: cited as evidence against August Wittfogel's "hydraulic empire" thesis, since their operation was controlled by monks rather than the state
Document: notes that women dominated all market trade in the empire
Document: describes commoners' homes as lacking tables, chairs, or bowls, with people wearing only loincloths
Document: describes an elaborate procession of one of the empire's rulers
Figure: served as an ambassador under the Yuan Chengzong Emperor before visiting and describing the empire
Document: describes a city surrounded by a moat guarded by a statue of a nine-headed snake
Event: a conflict-resolution ritual in which two disputing parties sat in stone towers in front of a palace until one fell ill
Institution: a caste system consisting of sdech (royalty), neamoeun montrey (nobility), and reastr (peasantry)
Figure: a legendary ruler was formerly a gardener known for his sweet cucumbers, until he assassinated his predecessor and married his daughter to take the throne
Figure: a king with an Oedipus-like backstory is said to have built the empire's first capital out of black sandstone
Event: a usurper ascended to rule by jumping onto the head of a rival's war elephant and killing him
Building: a seven-tier pyramid built by one of the empire's rulers at Koh Ker
Event: a ruler banned talking to trees as part of conservation efforts on Kulen Mountain
Site: hermits carved thousands of phallus-shaped carvings into limestone near the Kulen Plateau, later brought to archaeologists' attention by Jean Boulbet
Event: developed a martial art meaning "to pound a lion," whose fighters wear the krama scarf and appear on temple friezes
Event: fell due to invasions from the Ayutthaya Kingdom
Event: historian Bernard-Philippe Groslier argued its slow decline resulted from overdevelopment of a "hydraulic city"
Event: the drying out of two barays at the capital is cited as a major cause of the empire's collapse
Event: the Champa kingdom sacked the capital at Angkor about thirty years after the conquest of it by the builder of Angkor Wat
Event: the empire's collapse was followed by periods named for the cities of Chaktomuk and Longvek
Figure: his 19th-century expedition spurred Western interest in the empire's ruins, "discovering" Angkor despite it being known to natives for centuries
Figure: credited as the first Western visitor to Angkor
Figure: the first archaeologist to systematically study the empire, who noted a seven-tier pyramid at Koh Ker
Figure: compiled an 8-volume book of inscription transcriptions from the empire's ruins while working for the EFEO
Figure: used remote sensing technology to rediscover canals built by the empire
Event: the empire gives its name to a 20th-century communist party led by Pol Pot
